g.data-narration: Welcome to our exploration of Protection and Security in Operating Systems. Let us begin by understanding why these mechanisms matter.
g.text: Protection and Security in Operating Systems

g.data-narration: Protection is internal OS control, while Security is external defense using encryption and firewalls. Both work together to safeguard systems.
In multi-user environments, OS must protect resources from errors and malicious attacks, ensuring system integrity and data safety.
The Principle of Least Privilege states that processes should receive only the minimum privileges needed to complete their task.
g.text: Minimizes damage from errors or breaches
Example: User programs cannot access kernel memory

g.data-narration: A Protection Domain defines the access rights a process has. It includes objects like files and hardware, and rights like read, write, execute.
g.text: UNIX example: User ID defines domain

g.data-narration: The Access Matrix is a 2D table showing which domains can access which objects. Rows are domains, columns are objects, cells show rights.
This sample Access Matrix shows User1 can read/write File_A but only read Memory Segment. User2 has different permissions.
g.text: User_1 can R/W File_A, R Memory

g.data-narration: ACLs store permissions by object (column). Each file has a list of users and their access rights. Linux file permissions are a form of ACL.
g.text: Good for: Who can access this file?

g.data-narration: Capability Lists store permissions by domain (row). Each process has a list of capabilities (tokens) granting access to specific objects.
g.text: Good for: What can this process access?

g.data-narration: Authentication verifies identity as the first line of defense. Username/password combinations are compared against secure databases like /etc/shadow.
g.text: AUTHENTICATION - The First Line of Defense

g.data-narration: OS must guard against viruses that attach to programs, worms that self-propagate, Trojans disguised as legit software, and DoS attacks.
Key takeaways: Protection is internal OS control while Security is external. Least Privilege minimizes damage. Access Matrix conceptualizes rights.
These principles apply to system admins configuring permissions, developers writing secure code, and security analysts defending systems daily.
g.text: From smartphone login to online banking — OS protection works invisibly to protect you!
